retrieved_docs.title: Phenylketonuria -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Phenylketonuria (PKU) is an inborn error of metabolism (IEM) most often caused by missense mutations in the gene encoding phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH) which catalyzes (see Figure 1) the hydroxylation of phenylalanine (Phe) generating tyrosine (Tyr). [1] PKU belongs to a class of amino acid aminoacidopathies termed “toxic accumulation-IEMs” where the circulating toxin is an amino acid or its metabolites. Mutations in an enzyme, such as PAH, are recessive since one functioning enzyme with the wild-type allele is sufficient.

retrieved_docs.title: Phenylketonuria -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: As shown in the figure below, the conversion of Phe to Tyr by PAH requires BH4 and a deficiency of BH4 can cause hyperphenylalaninemia (HPA) even in the presence of wild-type PAH. If a newborn has a positive screen for HPA, further testing should be performed for pterins. Although beyond the scope of this review, it is possible to diagnose all forms of BH4 deficiencies based on the pattern of pterins.

retrieved_docs.title: Phenylketonuria -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: A recent review shows that some cognitive impairments can occur even with early PKU treatment with deficits noted in executive functions. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Phenylketonuria -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Phenylketonuria -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4) binds to the catalytic domain of PAH and is a cofactor for this reaction. See figure. PAH is primarily a hepatic enzyme. Elevated blood Phe levels and decreased Tyr levels characterize PKU. Newborns with PKU can appear normal at birth with the first signs appearing after several months. These signs can include musty odor from skin and urine, fair skin, eczema, seizures, tremors, and hyperactivity.

retrieved_docs.title: Phenylketonuria -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Explain how to counsel a patient and their family about lifestyle to decrease the risk of progression and complications of phenylketonuria. Describe the history and physical exam findings typically seen in patients with phenylketonuria. Explain the proper management of phenylketonuria. Describe how coordination of the interprofessional team can lead to more rapid diagnosis of phenylketonuria and subsequently decrease associated morbidity in affected patients. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Phenylketonuria -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Most PKU patients have 2 different PKU variants, i.e., they are compound heterozygotes. [3] PKU patients can, for example, have 2 severe alleles (severe/severe) or 1 severe allele and 1 mild allele (severe/mild). It is possible that some mild PAH gene variants code for an enzyme with reduced affinity for BH4 (a Km variant) and/or an enzyme variant with increased stability and half-life as a resulting of BH4 binding. Some 49% of PKU patients respond to oral BH4 supplementation with a 30% decrease in blood Phe levels despite having normal levels of BH4. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Phenylketonuria -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: PKU in the United States is detected by the state newborn screening program which measures the Phe/Tyr molar ratio on a filter paper blood spot (from a heal prick) by tandem mass spectrometry (MS/MS). This test is usually done 1 or 2 days after birth. Newborns with PKU can appear normal at birth with the first signs appearing after several months. Neonates with elevated blood Phe may have a BH4 deficiency which could also result in elevated Phe levels. Commercial microarray genotyping (for example, 23andme.com) for single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) can provide the carrier status for PKU variants common in those with Northern European descent. Knowing carrier status could be useful for future parents; if both are carriers of a PKU variant, there is a 25% of having a child with PKU.

retrieved_docs.title: Phenylketonuria -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: In the United States, PKU is usually detected at birth by newborn screening tests and dietary therapy started in consultation with a dietitian and geneticist/metabolism specialist. Mild forms of PKU in a newborn can, however, go undetected if the mother is discharged too soon or if the newborn has not consumed any protein. Signs and symptoms of the disease that become more pronounced when untreated include delayed developmental milestones, microcephaly, hypopigmentation, hyperactivity/behavior problems, seizures, and a musty odor to skin and urine. However, affected children who are diagnosed and appropriately treated at birth are less likely to show symptoms. Long-term care focuses on adherence to dietary therapy, monitoring of blood Phe and Tyr levels and assessing for potential cognitive impairments.

retrieved_docs.title: Phenylketonuria -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The overall incidence of PKU in the United States is about 1/15,000. This incidence is greater for Caucasian and Native American populations and less for African American, Hispanic and Asian populations. There is a large worldwide variation in PKU incidence. In Turkey, the incidence is particularly high with 1/4000 live births.

retrieved_docs.title: Phenylketonuria -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: When diagnosed early, classic PKU can be treated by life-long dietary therapy focused on maintaining low Phe levels and adequate Tyr intake.  These dietary interventions are generally effective at preventing the most severe cognitive impairment due to high Phe levels. Nevertheless, dietary therapy for PKU has been associated with deficiencies in selenium, copper, magnesium, and zinc.

retrieved_docs.title: Phenylketonuria -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: There are over 1000 mutations resulting in PKU, the most common replaces arginine (Arg) with tryptophan (Trp) at position 408 (i.e., Arg408Trp). Mutations in PAH causing PKU are generally due to a reduced PAH activity or a reduced PAH expression resulting in increased blood levels of Phe and decreased Tyr levels. Most PKU-causing PAH variants result from PAH misfolding and/or instability. [2] The Arg408Trp mutation results in “classic” PKU where blood Phe levels are greater than 1200 micromolar. Less severe mutations result in mild PKU, with blood Phe levels from 600 to 1200 micromolar, and mild hyperphenylalaninemia with blood Phe levels being less than 600 micromolar.  Phe is an essential amino acid, and its conversion to Tyr is normally well-regulated to provide sufficient levels for protein synthesis while maintaining levels sufficiently low to be nontoxic. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Phenylketonuria -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment of IEMs has traditionally relied on treating the nutritional and/or metabolic environment, for example, replacement enzyme therapy, rather than the genetic disorder itself. Rapid advances in gene therapy and its safety may one day be possible. [6] As of 2018, phase 1, gene-therapy trials for PKU remain in the planning stage.

retrieved_docs.title: Phenylketonuria -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Medical centers specializing in IEMs often have sufficient resources to provide both a team approach for PKU and extensive patient education programs.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Amino Acid Synthesis and Degradation -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The deficiency of Phe hydroxylase causes PKU. More than 400 mutations have been identified in the gene related to the cause of PKU. [28] This disease is usually heterozygous. Besides, the deficiency of dihydropteridine reductase (DHPR) or tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4) synthesis enzymes also leads to hyperphenylalaninemia. [29] [30] The BH4 or generated product replacement therapy treats this enzyme deficiency-related PKU. In the case of the classic PKU, the Phe, phenyl lactate, phenylpyruvate, and phenylacetate are increased in the plasma, urine, and other tissue samples. The phenyl pyruvic acid excreted in urine produces a "mousy" odor. Central nervous system symptoms, such as mental retardation, seizures, failure to walk or speak, tremors, and hyperactivity, also show in these patients. Another characteristic of classic PKU is hypopigmentation, which is due to the deficiency in the formation of melanin, which leads to pigmentation deficiency. Usually, the patients show light skin, fair hair, and blue eyes. Temporally, low Phe content synthetic nutrient supplemented with Tyr is the treatment of the classic PKU.

retrieved_docs.title: Phenylketonuria -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Phenylketonuria (PKU) is an inborn error of metabolism (IEM) most often caused by missense mutations in the gene encoding phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH), which catalyzes the hydroxylation of phenylalanine (Phe) to generate tyrosine (Tyr). PKU belongs to a class of aminoacidopathies termed toxic accumulation IEMs, in which the accumulation of an amino acid or its metabolite is toxic. Elevated blood Phe levels and decreased Tyr levels characterize PKU. Newborns with PKU can appear normal at birth with the first signs appearing after several months. These signs can include musty odor from skin and urine, fair skin, eczema, seizures, tremors, and hyperactivity. This activity examines the presentation, evaluation, and management of phenylketonuria and stresses the role of an interprofessional team approach to the care of affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 310
retrieved_docs.content: Aminokyseliny (amino acids) are classified as organické látky (organic substances) and belong to the broader group of kyseliny karboxylové (carboxylic acids). Within this class, prolin (proline) is identified as a specific parent member, and inverse_isa relationships link aminokyseliny to both kyselina 6-aminokapronová (6‑aminocaproic acid) and the general category of carboxylic acids, underscoring their chemical nature. Several more specialized entities are described as children or narrower members of aminokyseliny: chloromethylketony aminokyselin (chloromethyl ketone derivatives of amino acids), dihydroxyfenylalanin (dihydroxyphenylalanine, a specific amino acid derivative), and S‑adenosylhomocystein, a metabolite closely related to amino‑acid metabolism. The term Neurotransmitters & Neuroregulators (PsycINFO Subcluster Term) lists aminokyseliny as a member, highlighting the role of amino acids as precursors or components of neurotransmitter and neuroregulatory systems. Additionally, the compounds N.O.-Xplode, Tolpa, and pomaglumetad methionil are each mapped_to aminokyseliny, indicating that these agents are formulated from, derived from, or functionally related to amino‑acid structures. Together, these relationships portray amino acids as fundamental organic molecules that serve as building blocks for diverse biochemical pathways, therapeutic agents, and neurochemical functions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1762
retrieved_docs.content: S‑adenosylhomocysteine and S‑adenosylmethionine are metabolites that belong to the family of amino acids (aminokyseliny). S‑adenosylmethionine is classified as an isomeric form of an amino acid and serves as a precursor to adenosine, while S‑adenosylhomocysteine also gives rise to adenosine. Adenosine functions as a neurotransmitter and has a broader relationship with neurotransmitery. Amino acids in general are linked to neurotransmitters, acting as biochemical precursors. Within the amino‑acid class, chloromethyl‑ketone amino acids (chloromethylketony aminokyselin) are a subclass (child) of aminokyseliny. Proline (prolin) is identified as a parent member of the amino‑acid group. The network of amino acids is mapped to the experimental compound pomaglumetad methionil, indicating a pharmacological connection. This integrated view highlights the metabolic pathways from amino acids through S‑adenosyl‑ derivatives to adenosine and their roles in neurotransmission, as well as structural subclasses and drug‑mapping relationships.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1461
retrieved_docs.content: Amino Acid, Peptide, or Protein is the broad class that includes the entity aminokyseliny, which represents a group of amino‑acid‑related compounds. Within this group, specific derivatives such as chloromethylketony aminokyselin are identified as child members, indicating a more specialized chemical structure. Biochemically, aminokyseliny is linked to S‑adenosylhomocysteine, reflecting a metabolic relationship in which S‑adenosylhomocysteine participates in pathways involving these amino‑acid derivatives. The amino‑acid family is also connected to the individual amino acid prolin (proline), situating prolin as a parent concept that contextualizes aminokyseliny within the larger set of proteinogenic amino acids. In addition to these biochemical and structural links, the compounds Tolva and pomaglumetad methionil are mapped to aminokyseliny, suggesting that they are associated with, or act upon, this group of amino‑acid‑related molecules in therapeutic or research settings. Together, these relationships illustrate how aminokyseliny integrates hierarchical classification, metabolic connections, specific chemical variants, and pharmacological associations within the broader context of amino acids, peptides, and proteins.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1282
retrieved_docs.content: Aminokyseliny, the Czech term for amino acids, is presented as a broad biochemical class that encompasses several related entities. Within this hierarchy, chloromethylketony aminokyselin is identified as a child concept of aminokyseliny, indicating a more specific subclass or derivative of the general amino‑acid group. The specific compound kyselina 6‑aminokapronová (6‑aminocaproic acid) is described as an instance of aminokyseliny, illustrating that this particular acid belongs to the overall amino‑acid family. In addition, aminokyseliny is mapped to the pharmacological agent pomaglumetad methionil, suggesting a conceptual or functional association between the amino‑acid class and this drug‑like molecule. Finally, prolin is noted as a parent concept for aminokyseliny, reflecting a hierarchical organization in which prolin serves as a reference point or foundational category within the broader amino‑acid taxonomy. This integrated view highlights the interconnections among specific amino‑acid derivatives, individual acids, related therapeutic compounds, and hierarchical classifications that together inform a comprehensive understanding of amino‑acid biology and its relevance to medical research and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2750
retrieved_docs.content: Aminokyseliny (amino acids) represent a broad class of biologically important molecules, and within this class the specific amino acid prolin (proline) is included as one of its members. Specialized chemical derivatives known as chloromethylketony aminokyselin (chloromethyl ketones of amino acids) are formed from aminokyseliny, illustrating how amino acids can be chemically modified to create distinct sub‑structures. In addition, aminokyseliny are mapped to pomaglumetad methionil, indicating a biochemical or pharmacological association between the general pool of amino acids and this particular compound. Together, these relationships highlight the hierarchical organization of amino acids, their capacity for chemical derivatization, and their relevance to drug‑related research.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1446
retrieved_docs.content: Aminokyseliny (amino acids) are classified as organické látky (organic substances) and are a subset of kyseliny karboxylové (carboxylic acids), reflecting their characteristic carboxyl functional group. Within this broad class, individual amino acids such as prolin (proline) are specific members, and particular derivatives like kyselina 6‑aminokapronová (6‑aminocaproic acid) exemplify specialized forms. Further subcategories include chloromethylketony aminokyselin (chloromethyl ketone derivatives of amino acids), which represent a more specific chemical subgroup. In the field of neuroscience, aminokyseliny are recognized as members of the Neurotransmitters & Neuroregulators (PsycINFO Subcluster Term), highlighting their role as neurotransmitters and neuroregulatory compounds. Additionally, the product N.O.-Xplode is mapped to aminokyseliny, indicating that it is formulated based on or associated with amino acids. This network of relationships underscores the central position of amino acids in organic chemistry, physiology, and applied products.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 324
retrieved_docs.content: Aminokyseliny (amino acids) serve as a central hub linking a variety of chemical entities and classification structures. Both N.O.-Xplode and Tolpa are mapped to aminokyseliny, indicating that these items are categorized within the amino‑acid domain. The Neurotransmitters & Neuroregulators (PsycINFO Subcluster Term) lists aminokyseliny as a member, reflecting the role of amino acids as fundamental components of neurotransmitter and neuroregulator systems. S‑adenosylhomocystein is described as a child of aminokyseliny, positioning it as a metabolite derived from or closely related to amino acids. Aminokyseliny also has a child called chloromethylketony aminokyselin, denoting a more specific subclass of amino‑acid derivatives. Narrower relationships connect aminokyseliny to dihydroxyfenylalanin and levodopa, suggesting that these compounds are particular amino‑acid–based molecules with specialized functions. An inverse‑ISA relationship links aminokyseliny to kyselina 6‑aminokapronová, placing the latter as a specific acid within the broader amino‑acid class. Additionally, aminokyseliny is mapped to pomaglumetad methionil, associating this experimental compound with the amino‑acid category. Finally, prolin is identified as the parent of aminokyseliny, indicating that proline represents a foundational or prototypical amino acid within this hierarchical framework. Together, these connections illustrate how amino acids intersect with pharmacological agents, metabolic intermediates, and neurochemical classifications, underscoring their extensive relevance to biomedical understanding and patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: Aminokysely (amino acids) are presented as a central component of the Neurotransmitters & Neuroregulators subcluster, with prolin (proline) identified as a parent amino acid and several specific members such as chloromethylketony aminokyselin, kyselina 6‑aminokapronová (6‑aminocaproic acid), levodopa, and dihydroxyfenylalanin (L‑DOPA). These amino acids are linked to neurotransmitery (neurotransmitters) through a general relationship, indicating that many amino acids serve as precursors or structural elements of neurotransmitter molecules. Aminokysely are also mapped to the investigational compound pomaglumetad methionil, suggesting a pharmacological connection. Dopaminergic agents are classified as a broader subgroup of neurotransmitery and include several well‑known drugs: levodopa, karbidopa, dihydroxyfenylalanin (which is itself a dopaminergic agent), and the compound 1‑methyl‑4‑fenyl‑1,2,3,6‑tetrahydropyridin. N.O.-Xplode is mapped to the amino‑acid category (aminokyseliny), indicating that it is related to or derived from amino acids. Akamprosát is classified as an aminokyseliny, meaning it is considered a type of amino acid. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2428
retrieved_docs.content: S‑adenosylhomocysteine is linked to the broader class of amino acids (aminokyseliny) as a child entity, indicating that it is considered part of or derived from this group. Within the aminokyseliny hierarchy, chloromethylketony aminokyselin is identified as a subordinate compound, further expanding the spectrum of related amino‑acid derivatives. The compound known as 6‑aminocaproic acid (kyselina 6‑aminokapronová) is recognized as a specific member of the amino‑acid family, reflecting an inverse “is‑a” relationship that places it under the aminokyseliny classification. Proline (prolin) is described as a parent entity of the aminokyseliny class, indicating that many of the listed compounds are derived from or related to the proline scaffold. Together, these relationships illustrate how S‑adenosylhomocysteine, chloromethylketony aminokyselin, 6‑aminocaproic acid, and pomagulmetad methionil are interconnected within the biochemical context of amino‑acid chemistry, with proline serving as a foundational reference point for understanding their interrelations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2050
retrieved_docs.content: N.O.-Xplode is mapped to aminokyseliny, indicating that this entity is directly related to or derived from amino acids. S‑adenosylhomocystein is described as having a child relationship with aminokyseliny, suggesting that it functions as a biochemical precursor or upstream metabolite in pathways involving amino acids. Within the hierarchy of aminokyseliny, chloromethylketony aminokyselin is identified as a child, representing a specific subclass of modified amino acids that contain a chloromethyl ketone functional group. Levodopa is noted as having a narrower relationship to aminokyseliny, meaning that levodopa is a particular amino‑acid‑based compound used therapeutically, especially in the treatment of Parkinson’s disease. Finally, prolin (proline) is listed as a parent of aminokyseliny, reflecting its role as a representative or foundational amino acid from which broader categories of amino acids are conceptually derived. Together, these relationships illustrate a network in which N.O.-Xplode, S‑adenosylhomocystein, chloromethylketony aminokyselin, levodopa, and prolin are all interconnected through their associations with the central concept of aminokyseliny (amino acids).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1280
retrieved_docs.content: 1‑Methyl‑4‑fenyl‑1,2,3,6‑tetrahydropyridine and dihydroxyfenylalanine are both classified as dopaminergic agents. In addition, levodopa and karbidopa belong to the same class of dopaminergic agents. These compounds share a common therapeutic purpose of enhancing dopaminergic activity, either by serving as precursors that increase central dopamine synthesis (as with levodopa/dihydroxyfenylalanine) or by inhibiting peripheral metabolism of dopamine precursors (as with karbidopa). By recognizing that these chemically distinct agents all function as dopaminergic agents, clinicians can better understand and manage conditions characterized by dopamine deficiency, such as Parkinson’s disease, through appropriate selection and combination of these medications.

retrieved_docs.content: N.O.-Xplode, S‑adenosylhomocysteine, and Tolpa are all linked to the broader category of amino acids, indicating that each of these entities is either associated with or derived from amino‑acid chemistry. Within the amino‑acid hierarchy, proline is identified as a parent exemplar, serving as a reference point for the class of amino acids. The subclass of chloromethyl‑ketone amino acids is described as a child of the general amino‑acid group, highlighting a more specialized chemical family. Additionally, 6‑aminocaproic acid (kyselina 6‑aminokapronová) is related to amino acids through an inverse‑is‑a relationship, signifying that it is a specific type of amino‑acid derivative. The compound pomaglumetad methionil is also mapped to the amino‑acid domain, suggesting a pharmacological or structural connection to amino‑acid frameworks. Together, these relationships illustrate a network in which various drugs, metabolites, and chemical subclasses are interconnected through their association with, derivation from, or classification within the amino‑acid family, providing a comprehensive view of how these entities relate to fundamental amino‑acid biology and therapeutic chemistry.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 42
retrieved_docs.content: The category Amino Acids, I.V. solution additive comprises compounds such as alanylglutamine, which is a dipeptide and a member of this additive group. This additive category is linked to the Czech term aminokyseliny, reflecting its broader classification as amino acids. Aminokyseliny appear in several contexts: they are included as members of the Neurotransmitters & Neuroregulators (PsycINFO Subcluster Term), indicating a role in neurotransmission; they are related to the dietary supplement N.O.-Xplode and to the product Tolva, both of which map to aminokyseliny. Within the biochemical hierarchy, S‑adenosylhomocystein is described as having aminokyseliny as a child concept, and aminokyseliny in turn has more specific derivatives such as chloromethylketony aminokyselin, dihydroxyfenylalanin, and levodopa, the latter being a well‑known precursor of dopamine. The acid kyselina 6‑aminokapronová (6‑aminocaproic acid) is identified as a specific instance of aminokyseliny through an inverse‑is‑a relationship. The compound pomaglumetad methionil is also mapped to aminokyseliny, underscoring its classification as an amino‑acid‑derived molecule. Prolin (proline) is noted as a parent concept within the amino‑acid family, situating aminokyseliny within the broader spectrum of proteinogenic amino acids. Additionally, the Amino Acids, I.V. solution additive category is associated with Antifibrinolytic amino acids, a subgroup that falls under the class of antifibrinolytika, highlighting a therapeutic application in inhibiting fibrinolysis. Together, these relationships illustrate the multifaceted roles of amino‑acid‑related compounds in nutrition, pharmacology, neurobiology, and hemostasis.
